statusLine: European fuel stocks are under strain with 19 of 27 EU countries below the mandatory 90-day minimum in at least one fuel category, while oil prices have surged 20.5% since February's Middle East escalation.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as the majority of EU countries struggle to maintain adequate reserves across all fuel types. Of the 27 EU nations, 19 countries have critical shortfalls in at least one fuel category, falling below the mandatory 90-day minimum for net imports. The situation is particularly acute for jet fuel, where 16 countries report critical levels, and diesel, affecting 15 countries.

The geographic distribution of shortages reveals concerning patterns. Eastern European nations like Luxembourg (38 days petrol), Poland (53.2 days petrol), and Romania face severe deficits across multiple fuel types. Major economies are also affected, with France holding just 69.3 days of petrol reserves and Italy maintaining only 61.4 days of diesel stocks. Germany, despite better overall reserves, shows critical jet fuel levels at 59.9 days.

Oil price pressures compound these supply challenges. Brent crude has climbed from $72.48 to $87.33 per barrel since the February Middle East escalation, representing a 20.5% increase that translates directly into higher fuel costs across the continent. This price surge, combined with widespread strategic reserve releases following supply disruptions, has created a dual challenge of both availability and affordability for European consumers and businesses.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries have critical shortfalls below the 90-day minimum in at least one fuel category
Jet fuel shows the most widespread vulnerability with 16 countries at critical levels, followed by diesel affecting 15 countries
Major economies including France, Italy, and parts of Germany face significant reserve deficits despite their size
Brent crude oil has surged 20.5% since February's Middle East escalation, from $72.48 to $87.33 per barrel
Price variations across the EU remain substantial, with Danish petrol at €2.39/L compared to Malta's €1.34/L
